verses.text: ‘Elihu spake moreover, and said.’ Each of Elihu’s speeches gets its own opening (Job 34, 35, 36). The structure marks them as distinct theological arguments. Across these chapters Elihu will press one question harder than the friends did: what kind of God is God in the face of suffering? His answer points toward divine pedagogy — God teaching even through hardship.

Elihu challenges Job’s implicit claim that his righteousness was superior to God’s justice. The verse names the danger of self-justification: in defending ourselves too vigorously, we can drift into accusing God. Even legitimate complaint can curdle into pride if it goes unchecked. Elihu does not deny Job’s suffering; he questions Job’s framing.

‘What profit shall I have, if I be cleansed from my sin?’ Elihu paraphrases what Job has implied: that righteousness has yielded him nothing. The challenge cuts deep. Sometimes we measure faith by what it produces for us. Elihu names this and pushes back. Righteousness is not a transaction with God; it is the right shape of a life before Him.

35:4-8 Elihu attempted to answer his own question (35:2), but his answer did not add to what Zophar (11:8-9), Eliphaz (22:12), Bildad (25:5-6), and Job (9:8-10) had already said. Elihu failed to recognize that all sin wrongs God (Gen 39:9; Matt 25:41-46; cp. Prov 19:17).

35:15-16 Elihu echoed and then rejected Job’s words (9:24; 12:6-10; 21:7-15, 17).
35:16 Elihu echoed his earlier accusation (34:35); later, God would agree (38:2).
